data.source: World Values Survey (WVS) for political preference questions; macro fiscal measures of government size (GOVCONS, GOVTRANSSUB); election results primarily from Beck et al. (2001).
data.wave_or_subset: Mostly the 2000 WVS wave, supplemented by the 1995 wave; polarization computed from four economic policy questions. Analysis covers 74 countries.
data.sample_size: Approximately 1,000 face-to-face respondents per country in the WVS; 74 countries included in the main analysis.
data.notes: Polarization measured mainly as the standard deviation of responses to four 1–10 economic policy questions (equality, private vs. government ownership, government responsibility, competition). Potential biases due to question context and language acknowledged. Polarization shown relatively stable over time (correlations 0.72–0.81 across WVS waves). Alternative polarization metrics (e.g., Esteban and Ray, bipolarization) used for robustness. Measurement error may be larger in weak democracies. Dependent variables include general government consumption (share of total consumption) and transfers/subsidies (share of GDP), often averaged over 2003–2005.

method.description: The study constructs country-level polarization measures from WVS responses to four economic policy questions and estimates cross-country regressions of government size on polarization. Analyses are run for all countries and separately for weak versus strong democracies, with multiple control specifications and robustness checks including alternative polarization measures, additional controls (e.g., inequality, trust, corruption), and interaction terms (e.g., legislative fragmentation, government seat share). Factor analysis is used to separate preferences for the size versus composition of spending.
steps: Compile WVS responses for four 1–10 economic policy questions and compute country-level polarization (primarily as standard deviation) and composite measures.
Assemble macro outcomes: general government consumption share (GOVCONS) and transfers/subsidies share (GOVTRANSSUB), typically averaged over 2003–2005.

Classify countries by democratic strength and collect additional covariates (e.g., GDP per capita, trade openness, geographic/colonial controls, demographics, inequality, trust/confidence, corruption, fragmentation indices).
Estimate cross-country OLS regressions of government size on polarization with basic, short, and long control specifications; run models for all countries, weak democracies, and strong democracies.

Conduct robustness checks: include mean response controls, ideological coherence, consensus/response uncertainty, fractionalization indices, regime-type indicators; test alternative polarization metrics (Esteban–Ray, bipolarization).

Explore mechanisms via interactions with legislative/government fragmentation and government seat share; analyze polarization among government vs. opposition supporters; use factor analysis to distinguish size vs. composition preferences.
Assess stability and potential endogeneity concerns; compare results across specifications and subsamples.
method.models: Cross-country OLS regressions of the form y_i = α + β Polarization_i + X_i γ + ε_i, including interactions with fragmentation indices (HERFGOV, HERFLEG) and government seat share (GOVSEATS).
method.outcome_variable: Government size measures: general government consumption as a share of total consumption (GOVCONS); government transfers and subsidies as a share of GDP (GOVTRANSSUB); redistribution-related outcomes.
method.independent_variables: Primary: polarization of self-reported economic policy preferences measured as the standard deviation of responses to four WVS questions (equality, private vs. government ownership, government responsibility for welfare (GOV), competition) and a composite measure (SD_COMP). Alternative polarization measures include Esteban–Ray and bipolarization.
method.control_variables: Mean response to policy questions; GDP per capita; trade openness; demographic factors; geographic and colonial origin dummies; income inequality (Gini); ethnic/other fractionalization indices; trust and confidence measures (e.g., confidence in parliament); corruption; press freedom; regime type indicators; ideological coherence (correlation across policy responses); consensus/response uncertainty (absolute deviation from midpoint 5.5); legislative/government fragmentation (HERFLEG, HERFGOV); number of parties; government seat share.

results.summary: Political polarization is negatively associated with government size, especially in strong democracies. Across multiple specifications and polarization measures, higher polarization predicts lower general government consumption and, in long specifications, lower redistribution. The relationship is robust to numerous controls (e.g., GDP per capita, inequality, trust, corruption) and alternative polarization metrics. Effects are small or insignificant in weak democracies, suggesting measurement issues there. Interaction analyses provide mixed support for mechanisms involving fragmentation and incumbency strategy; polarization among government supporters correlates more strongly with spending than polarization among opposition supporters.
